era_context: Early years of the English Restoration under Charles II (1660–1685); the Qing Dynasty consolidating Manchu rule in China; the Ottoman Empire in the early phase of the Köprülü Vizierate (1656–1703), a period of administrative reform and military recovery; European colonial expansion in the Americas and Asia accelerating.

events.title: Charles II marries Catherine of Braganza; Portuguese alliance
events.description: King Charles II of England married Catherine of Braganza, daughter of King John IV of Portugal, on 21 May 1662. The marriage was a diplomatic alliance that strengthened English-Portuguese relations and brought a substantial dowry to England, including the territories of Tangier and Bombay (Mumbai). Catherine's marriage was politically significant but personally unhappy, as Charles maintained numerous mistresses. The marriage produced no legitimate heirs, which would have long-term consequences for English succession. The dowry territories, particularly Bombay, became important bases for English colonial expansion in Asia. The marriage was celebrated in England as a triumph of Restoration diplomacy.

sources.name: Contemporary English court records and diplomatic correspondence
Samuel Pepys, Diary, entries for May 1662

events.certainty_note: The marriage date (21 May 1662) is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Act of Uniformity enforced in England; religious conformity required
events.description: The Act of Uniformity of 1662 came into force on 24 August 1662 (St. Bartholomew's Day), requiring all English clergy to conform to the Book of Common Prayer and the established Church of England. The act resulted in the ejection of approximately 2,000 Nonconformist ministers who refused to conform, an event known as the Great Ejection. This was a major blow to Puritan and Presbyterian clergy who had gained influence during the Interregnum (1649–1660). The act was part of the Restoration's effort to re-establish Anglican orthodoxy and royal authority over the church. The ejections created a dissenting religious community that would persist and grow throughout the late 17th century.

sources.name: Contemporary ecclesiastical records and clergy lists
Richard Baxter and other ejected ministers' memoirs

events.certainty_note: The Act of Uniformity and the Great Ejection are well-documented in ecclesiastical and state records.

events.title: Zheng Chenggong (Koxinga) dies; Ming loyalist resistance weakens
events.description: Zheng Chenggong (known to Europeans as Koxinga), the Ming loyalist general and pirate leader, died on 23 June 1662. Zheng had resisted Qing rule and maintained a powerful naval force based in Taiwan and along the Chinese coast. His death marked a turning point in the Qing's consolidation of control over southern China and Taiwan. Zheng's son, Zheng Jing, inherited his father's position but lacked his military genius and political acumen. The Qing would gradually reduce the Zheng family's power, culminating in their surrender in 1683. Zheng Chenggong's death is a significant moment in the transition from Ming loyalist resistance to Qing dominance in East Asia.

sources.name: Qing court records and military reports
Ming loyalist chronicles and later compilations

events.certainty_note: Zheng Chenggong's death date (23 June 1662) is well-documented in Qing and Zheng family records.

events.title: Royal Society receives royal charter; institutional establishment
events.description: The Royal Society of London received its formal royal charter from King Charles II in 1662, formally establishing it as the premier scientific institution in England. The Society had been meeting informally since 1660, but the 1662 charter gave it legal status and royal patronage. The charter defined the Society's mission as the advancement of natural knowledge through experimentation and observation. The Society became a centre for the Scientific Revolution in England, hosting meetings where natural philosophers like Robert Boyle, John Evelyn, and others presented their work. The charter was a significant moment in the institutionalization of science in Europe.
key_figures: Henry Oldenburg (Secretary of the Royal Society)

sources.name: Royal charter of the Royal Society, 1662
Royal Society records and meeting minutes

events.certainty_note: The royal charter date and content are well-documented.

events.title: Blaise Pascal dies; legacy of mathematical and theological work
events.description: Blaise Pascal, the French mathematician, physicist, and theologian, died on 19 August 1662 at age 39. Pascal had made significant contributions to mathematics (Pascal's triangle, probability theory), physics (barometric pressure, hydraulics), and theology (the Pensées, a defence of Christian faith). His death marked the end of a remarkably productive intellectual life, though much of his work was published posthumously. Pascal's influence on the Scientific Revolution and on Christian apologetics was profound and lasting. His work bridged mathematics, natural philosophy, and theology, exemplifying the intellectual ambitions of the 17th century.
events.certainty_note: Pascal's death date (19 August 1662) is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Catherine of Braganza's marriage as a triumph for England is complicated by the fact that the dowry, while substantial, was not fully paid and the territories (particularly Tangier) proved difficult to hold and defend. Catherine's inability to produce an heir was a significant political failure, though not her personal fault. The Act of Uniformity is sometimes portrayed as a simple restoration of Anglican orthodoxy, but recent scholarship emphasizes the violence of the ejections and the creation of a lasting dissenting community that would challenge Anglican hegemony. Zheng Chenggong's death is sometimes attributed to illness, but some sources suggest suicide or assassination; the exact cause is debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1662 is rich in European sources, particularly English and French, reflecting the abundance of court records and diplomatic correspondence. Chinese sources are well-preserved but often filtered through Qing official narratives that may downplay Ming loyalist resistance. The year is well-attested in European intellectual and political history but less so in other regions. The significance of events is weighted toward European political and religious history; scientific developments are well-documented but may overshadow equally important developments in Asia. The Great Ejection is well-documented in English ecclesiastical records but the experience of ejected ministers and their communities is less thoroughly preserved.

graph_edges.note: Charles II's marriage to Catherine of Braganza brought Bombay to England, facilitating later colonial expansion
Act of Uniformity created dissenting community that influenced the Glorious Revolution and Toleration Act
Zheng Chenggong's death weakened Ming loyalist resistance, leading to Zheng family surrender in 1683
Royal Society charter established institutional framework for Newton's later work
